item.title: Web 组件：构建用户界面的革命性方法 | Web Components: A Revolutionary Way to Build User Interfaces
item.description:
以前，编码前端应用程序更加混乱，并且没有任何模块化方法可以将某些功能与其余代码分开。 这将导致 Web 开发采用更加模块化的方法，从而更容易阅读、构建和维护复杂的 Web 应用程序。

虽然我们有外部库/框架，如 react 和 angular，这将简化我们的工作，但为什么我们需要 Web 组件？

  • 这意味着未来所有主流浏览器都将支持它们。 在Can I Use Web Components查看当前的兼容性
  • 这意味着它们易于重用且易于维护。
  • 您无需学习新的库或框架即可使用它们。
  • 此外，这些与任何框架兼容。 写一次，随处使用。

创建 Web 组件很简单。 您所需要的只是对 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript 有基本的了解。 在本教程中，我们将介绍一些理解和创建 Web 组件的基本标准。

让我们开始吧（简介）：

通过使用 Web 组件，您可以保持代码干净和可维护，并创建可在任何网页或 Web 应用程序中使用的自定义元素。 四个主要标准是：

  • 自定义元素：允许开发人员创建自己的 HTML 标签。
  • 自定义元素：

    自定义元素的主要需求是为开发人员提供一种创建自己的 HTML 标记的方法。 这很重要，因为它允许开发人员扩展 HTML 词汇表，使其更具表现力和功能。 此外，自定义元素可以帮助创建更具语义意义的标记，并且它们可以通过将复杂的功能封装到单个可重用组件中来使代码更易于维护。 您可以创建自定义元素，例如等。

    有两种类型的自定义元素：

    • 自治自定义元素是不基于任何现有 HTML 元素的自定义元素。 它们是从头开始创建的，可以扩展任何其他自定义元素或标准 HTML 元素（通常是 HTMLElement 类）。

    • 自定义内置元素是基于现有 HTML 元素的自定义元素。 它们扩展了内置元素的功能，并可用于自定义该元素的行为或外观。

    在本教程中，我们将讨论自治自定义元素。 创建自治自定义元素很简单。 您需要做的就是创建一个扩展 HTMLElement 的 JavaScript 类。 然后可以将此类作为自定义元素注册到浏览器中。 这个类可以有许多方法，这些方法将在下面讨论。

    创建类后，您需要在浏览器中将其注册为自定义元素。 这是使用 customElements.define() 方法完成的。 此方法有两个参数：自定义元素的名称和表示自定义元素的类。

    注册自定义元素后，您可以像使用任何其他 HTML 元素一样使用它。 这里的所有都是它的！

    这是一个简单的自定义元素的架构，其中每个方法都是可选的：

    注意：自定义元素名称应包含- （连字符）以确保它们不会在自定义元素和普通 HTML 元素之间产生任何冲突。

     class MyElement extends HTMLElement { constructor() { super(); /* ... */ } connectedCallback() { /* ... */ } disconnectedCallback() { /* ... */ } static get observedAttributes() { return [/* ... */]; } attributeChangedCallback(name, oldValue, newValue) { /* ... */ } adoptedCallback() { /* ... */ } } customElements.define('my-element', MyElement);

    上述方法的功能：

    采用Callback ：当元素移动到新文档时调用，发生在 document.adoptNode 中，很少使用）

    现在，让我们举个例子来创建一个问候元素

    class Greeting extends HTMLElement { constructor() { super(); } connectedCallback() { this.innerHTML = `<p> Hello, ${this.getAttribute('name')} </p>`; } } customElements.define('greet-element', Greeting);

    这就是它的全部！ 您现在已经创建了一个可重用的 Web 组件，它可以在您的网页上的任何位置使用。

     <greet-element name="Arjun"></greet-element>

    这是输出的样子：

    影子 DOM：

    你有没有想过浏览器如何处理输入标签、选择标签等元素背后的复杂性。浏览器使用 DOM/CSS 在页面上绘制元素。 这种 DOM 结构通常对我们隐藏，但我们可以在开发人员工具中看到它。 例如，在 Chrome 中，我们需要在开发工具中启用“显示用户代理影子 DOM”选项。

    这是启用该选项并检查输入元素时的外观： 您在#shadow-root (user-agent) 下看到的内容称为影子 DOM。 像 input 和 select 这样的元素实现了内置的 shadow dom。 我们无法通过常规的 JavaScript 调用或选择器获取内置的影子 DOM 元素。

    这个 Shadow DOM 将与 Lighter DOM（普通 DOM）完全隔离。 有自己的 ids 空间、样式并且对外部文档不可见（较轻的 dom 选择器）。

    创建 Shadow DOM 很简单。 首先创建一个简单的 DOM 元素 elem，然后使用语法shadowRoot = elem.attachShadow({mode: open|closed})附加 shadom dom。 如果 mode="open"，那么它可以作为 elem.shadowRoot 属性访问。 如果 mode="close"，则 elem.shadowRoot 将为空。 我们现在可以使用像 innerHTML 或任何其他 DOM 方法这样的方法来填充这个shadowRoot元素。

    自定义元素可以使用 Shadow DOM 来封装组件内部并应用组件本地样式。 让我们看一个使用 shadow dom 的示例，用于上述自定义元素greet-element示例：

     class Greeting extends HTMLElement { constructor() { super(); } connectedCallback() { const shadow = this.attachShadow({mode: 'open'}); shadow.innerHTML = `<p> Hello, ${this.getAttribute('name')} </p>`; } } customElements.define('greet-element', Greeting);

    现在你可以在 HTML 中使用这个greet-element ，如下所示

    <greet-element name="Arjun"></greet-element>

    这是输出的样子：

    在创建网站或 Web 应用程序时，HTML 模板非常强大和有用。 它们提供了一种很好的方式来组织和组织代码，使其更具可读性和可维护性。 在插槽的帮助下，您可以进一步自定义模板的行为和外观。

    我们可以使用<template>标签编写模板，其中的内容可以是任何语法正确的 HTML。 我们还可以将样式和脚本放入<template>中。 浏览器将模板内容视为文档外的内容，因此在我们将模板附加到 DOM 的某个元素之前，不会运行样式、脚本或任何其他内容。

    模板的内容在其 content 属性中作为 DocumentFragment 提供。 这是一种特殊类型的 DOM 节点，可以像任何其他 DOM 节点一样对待，除了一个特殊属性：当它被插入某处时，它的子节点被插入。 例如考虑以下代码：

     <template id="templateExample"> <script> alert("Hello"); </script> <style> .greet { color: red; } </style> <div class="greet">Hello, world!</div> </template> <div id="output"></div> <script> // Clone the template content to reuse it multiple times output.append(templateExample.content.cloneNode(true)); // Now the script and styles from <template> runs </script>

    输出看起来像：

    插槽

    插槽就像模板中的占位符元素，可以填充不同的内容。 这种灵活性使您可以对模板的呈现方式进行大量控制。 例如，您可以使用插槽为不同的屏幕尺寸创建不同版本的模板。 或者您可以使用它们来更改模板元素的呈现顺序。

    有两种类型的插槽：命名插槽和未命名插槽。

    • 命名槽用于指定要填充内容的特定槽。 另一方面，当您不需要专门针对特定插槽（或者您希望所有剩余内容占据该位置）时，使用未命名插槽

    您可以看到这里使用了 2 种类型的插槽（请参阅 HTML 代码中的 #1、#2 注释）。 第一个插槽使用用户名，如果您没有从自定义元素中获取用户名，它将使用world！ 作为默认文本。 第二个槽是默认槽（即没有名称），它消耗所有剩余的孩子。 如果存在多个默认插槽，则仅考虑第一个，并忽略所有后续未命名的插槽。

    输出 ：

    从上面的输出中你可以看到插槽仍然在相同的位置并且没有移动到影子 DOM，但它仍然呈现在浏览器 DOM 上。 这种在槽内渲染槽元素的过程称为合成。 结果称为扁平化 DOM。

    你知道导入 JS 模块或 CSS 文件吗？ 导入 HTML 文件怎么样？ 有 HTML 导入。 这对于创建可在您的网站或 Web 应用程序中使用的可重用组件很有用。 例如，您可以创建一个包含导航菜单的组件，然后使用 HTML 导入将该组件包含在您网站的每个页面上。

    包含 HTML 导入类似于使用<link>元素加载 CSS 样式表，或使用<script>元素加载 JavaScript 文件。

    要包含 HTML 导入，请使用 rel 属性值为“import”的<link>元素：

     <link rel="import" href="/path/to/import.html">

    一旦 HTML 导入包含在文档中，浏览器将获取导入的文档并对其进行处理。

    如果导入的文档包含任何<link>或<script>元素，这些资源也将被加载和处理。

    处理导入的文档时，将处理任何元素并将内容添加到主文档中。 任何<script>元素都会被执行。

    处理 HTML 导入后，将更新生成的文档以反映所做的任何更改。

    尽管它们已被弃用，但您仍然可以通过为其添加 polyfill 来使用它们。

    弃用 HTML 导入

    但大多数浏览器拒绝了它。 常见的原因是 HTML 导入是多余的，因为您仍然需要 JavaScript 才能使这些东西运行。

    但是这个已弃用的 HTML 导入有一个后继者，即 HTML 模块。 要了解有关该提案的更多信息，请在此HTML 模块解释器中获取战利品

    结论 ：

    我希望您了解 Web 组件的基础知识和各种概念，如自定义元素、Shadow DOM、模板、插槽、HTML 导入、HTML 模块等。这只是冰山上的一个小窍门。 要了解有关这些概念的更多信息，请在每个主题上找到以下参考资料。

    自定义元素、 Shadow DOM 、模板和插槽、 HTML 模块解释器。

    另外，请在Can I Use Web Components中查看不同浏览器对 Web Components 的支持。

    还有一些库可以让开发 Web 组件变得更容易，一些领先的和我最喜欢的库是Lit和Stencil 。

    你真棒，你来了这么久。 如果你喜欢这篇文章，请告诉我。



原文:


Previously, coding frontend applications was more cluttered and there wasn't any modular approach to separating certain functionality from the rest of the code. Web components will allow developers to create reusable custom elements that can be used throughout their web applications. This will lead to a more modular approach to web development, making it easier to read, build and maintain complex web applications.

Though we got external libraries/frameworks like react and angular which will ease our work, why do we need Web Components?

  • Web components are standards-based. This means that they will be supported by all major browsers in the future. Check the current compatibility here at Can I Use Web Components
  • Web components are self-contained. This means that they are easy to reuse and easy to maintain.
  • Web components are easy to use. You don't need to learn a new library or framework to use them.
  • Additionally, these are compatible with any framework. Write once use anywhere.

Creating a web component is simple. All you need is a basic understanding of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. In this tutorial, we'll walk through a few of the basic standards of understanding and creating web components.

Let's get started (Introduction):

Web components are a set of W3C standards that allow developers to create reusable components for the web. By using web components, you can keep your code clean and maintainable, and create custom elements that can be used in any web page or web application. The four main standards are:

  • Custom Elements: Allows developers to create their own HTML tags.
  • Shadow DOM: Gives developers the ability to encapsulate DOM and CSS in a web component so that it is isolated from the rest of the document.
  • HTML Templates: Allows developers to create reusable HTML templates.
  • HTML Imports ( Deprecated ): Allows developers to import HTML documents into other HTML documents.

Custom Elements:

The main need for custom elements is to provide a way for developers to create their own HTML tags. This is important because it allows developers to extend the HTML vocabulary, making it more expressive and powerful. Additionally, custom elements can help create more semantically meaningful markup, and they can make code more maintainable by encapsulating complex functionality into a single, reusable component. You could create custom elements like , etc.

There are two types of Custom Elements:

  • Autonomous Custom Elements are custom elements that are not based on any existing HTML element. They are created from scratch and can extend any other custom element or standard HTML element ( generally HTMLElement class ).

  • Customized Built-in Elements are custom elements that are based on existing HTML elements. They extend the functionality of the built-in element and can be used to customize the behavior or appearance of that element.

In this tutorial, we will discuss Autonomous Custom Element. Creating a Autonomous Custom Element is simple. All you need to do is create a JavaScript class that extends HTMLElement. This class can then be registered with the browser as a custom element. This class can have many methods which are discussed below.

Once you have your class created, you need to register it with the browser as a custom element. This is done using the customElements.define() method. This method takes two arguments: the name of the custom element, and the class that represents the custom element.

After your custom element is registered, you can use it just like any other HTML element. That's all there is to it!

Here's the schema of a simple custom element, where every method is optional :

Note : custom element name should contain -(hyphen) to ensure their wont be any conflicts between custom elements and normal HTML elements.

class MyElement extends HTMLElement {
  constructor() { super(); /* ... */ }
  connectedCallback() { /* ... */ }
  disconnectedCallback() { /* ... */ }
  static get observedAttributes() { return [/* ... */]; }
  attributeChangedCallback(name, oldValue, newValue) { /* ... */ }
  adoptedCallback() { /* ... */ }
}

customElements.define('my-element', MyElement);

Functionalities of Above Methods :

connectedCallback : browser calls this method when the element is added to the document (can be called many times if an element is repeatedly added/removed)

disconnectedCallback : browser calls this method when the element is removed from the document

static get observedAttributes : returns array of attribute names to monitor for changes

attributeChangedCallback : called when one of attributes listed above in observedAttributes method is modified

adoptedCallback : called when the element is moved to a new document happens in document.adoptNode, very rarely used)

Now, lets take an example to create a greeting element

class Greeting extends HTMLElement {
  constructor() { super(); }
  connectedCallback() { 
    this.innerHTML = `<p>
      Hello, ${this.getAttribute('name')}
    </p>`;
 }
}

customElements.define('greet-element', Greeting);

And that's all there is to it! You've now created a reusable web component that can be used anywhere on your web page.

<greet-element name="Arjun"></greet-element>

This is how the output looks like :

Shadow DOM :

Have you ever wondered how the browser handles complexity behind elements like input tag, select tag etc. The browser uses DOM/CSS to draw elements on the page. This DOM structure is normally hidden from us, but we can see it in developer tools. For example, in Chrome, we need to enable the "Show user agent shadow DOM" option in Dev Tools.

This is how it looks when you enable that option and inspect the input element : What you see under #shadow-root (user-agent) is called shadow DOM. Elements like input and select implement built-in shadow dom. We cant get built-in shadow DOM elements by regular JavaScript calls or selectors.

This Shadow DOM will be completely isolated from Lighter DOM ( normal DOM). Has its own ids space, styles and is invisible to outer document (lighter dom selectors).

Creating Shadow DOM is simple . First create a simple DOM element elem and then attach shadom dom using the syntax shadowRoot = elem.attachShadow({mode: open|closed}). If mode="open", then its accessible as elem.shadowRoot property. If mode="closed", then elem.shadowRoot will be null. We can now populate this shadowRoot element using methods like innerHTML or any other DOM methods.

Custom Elements can use Shadow DOM to encapsulate component internals and apply component-local styles. Lets see an example using shadow dom for the above custom element greet-element example :

class Greeting extends HTMLElement {
  constructor() { super(); }
  connectedCallback() { 
    const shadow = this.attachShadow({mode: 'open'});
    shadow.innerHTML = `<p>
      Hello, ${this.getAttribute('name')}
    </p>`;
 }
}

customElements.define('greet-element', Greeting);

Now you can use this greet-element in HTML as follows

<greet-element name="Arjun"></greet-element>

This is how the output looks like :

HTML Templates :

HTML templates are very powerful and useful when it comes to creating websites or web applications. They provide a great way to structure and organize your code, making it more readable and maintainable. And with the help of slots, you can further customize the behavior and appearance of your template.

we can write Templates using <template> tag, where the content can be any syntactically correct HTML. We can also put styles and scripts into <template>. The browser considers template content as out of document, due to which styles, scripts or any other content is not run until we attach template to some element of DOM.

The content of the template is available as a DocumentFragment in its content property. This is a special type of DOM node that can be treated like any other DOM node, except for one special property: when it is inserted somewhere, its children are inserted instead. For example consider the following code :

<template id="templateExample">
  <script>
    alert("Hello");
  </script>
  <style>
    .greet {
      color: red;
    }
  </style>
  <div class="greet">Hello, world!</div>
</template>
<div id="output"></div>

<script>
   // Clone the template content to reuse it multiple times
   output.append(templateExample.content.cloneNode(true));
   // Now the script and styles from <template> runs
</script>

Output looks like :

Slots

Slots are like placeholder elements in a template that can be filled with different content. This flexibility gives you a lot of control over how your template is rendered. For example, you can use slots to create different versions of a template for different screen sizes. Or you can use them to change the order in which template elements are rendered.

There are two types of slots: named slots and unnamed slots.

  • Named slots are used to specify a particular slot that you want to fill with content. <slot name="X">...</slot> gets children with slot="X".
  • Unnamed slots, on the other hand, are used when you don't need to specifically target a particular slot ( or you want all the remaining content to take this spot )

Slots are better used when combined with remaining Web Components, so let's combine all the above concepts (custom elements, shadow DOM, templates) and write a simple usage of slot

<template id="greetTemplate">
  <style>
  .greet{
    color:red;
  }
  </style>
  <div class="greet">Hello, <slot name="user-name">World!</slot></div> <!-- #(1) -->
  <div>
    Remaining Content : <slot></slot> <!-- #(2) -->
  </div>
</template>

<greet-element>
  <span slot="user-name">Veeresh</span>
  Some Remaining Content,<b> More content </b>
</greet-element>


class Greeting extends HTMLElement {
  constructor() { super(); }
  connectedCallback() { 
    const shadow = this.attachShadow({mode: 'open'});
    const template = document
        .getElementById('greetTemplate')
        .content;
    shadow.append(template.cloneNode(true));
 }
}

customElements.define('greet-element', Greeting);

You can see 2 types of slots being used here ( refer to #1, #2 comments in HTML code). The first slot takes user-name, if you dont get user-name from custom element, it uses world! as default text. The second slot is the default slot ( i.e without a name ), which consumes all the remaining children. If there exists multiple default slots, only the first one is considered and all subsequent unnamed slots are ignored.

Output :

From the above output you can see that the slots are still at same position and haven't moved to shadow DOM, but still it renders on the browser DOM. This process of rendering slotted elements inside their slots is called composition. The result is called a flattened DOM.

HTML Imports :

You know importing JS modules or CSS files right? How about importing HTML files? There comes HTML Imports. HTML Imports is a feature of the Web Components specification that allows you to include HTML documents inside other HTML documents.

This is useful for creating reusable components that can be used across your website or web application. For example, you could create a component that contains a navigation menu, and then use the HTML Import to include that component on every page of your website.

HTML Imports are also useful for loading external resources, such as CSS stylesheets or JavaScript libraries, that are required by your web components.

Including an HTML Import is similar to using the <link> element to load a CSS stylesheet, or the <script> element to load a JavaScript file.

To include an HTML Import, you use the <link> element with a rel attribute value of "import":

<link rel="import" href="/path/to/import.html">

The href attribute value is the URL of the HTML document that you want to import.

Once an HTML Import is included in a document, the browser will fetch the imported document and process it.

If the imported document contains any <link> or <script> elements, those resources will also be loaded and processed.

When the imported document is processed, any elements will be processed and the contents will be added to the main document. Any <script> elements will be executed.

After the HTML Import is processed, the resulting document will be updated to reflect any changes that were made.

HTML Imports are a powerful way to create reusable components and load external resources. Though they are deprecated, you can still use them by including a polyfill for it.

Deprecation of HTML Imports

HTML Imports was really an exciting feature and it makes the web development truly modular. But most of the browsers rejected it. The common reason is HTML Imports were redundant, as you still need JavaScript to make the stuff run.

But there exists a successor to this deprecated HTML Imports, that is HTML Modules. HTML Modules are the new proposal for importing HTML files into a document. To know more about the proposal please have a loot at this HTML Modules Explainer

Conclusion :

I hope you got to know the basics of Web Components and various concepts like Custom Elements, Shadow DOMs, Templates, Slots, HTML Imports, HTML Modules, etc. This is just a tip on top of an iceberg. To know more about the concepts please find the below references on each topic.

Custom Elements, Shadow DOM,Templates and Slots,HTML Modules Explainer .

Also, check out the support of Web Components by different browsers here at Can I Use Web Components.

There also exist a few libraries that make developing web components easier, a few of the leading and my favorite libraries are Lit and Stencil.

You are awesome, you came this long. If you do like this post please let me know.
